verdict.functional_identity: PASS — 0.0000 accuracy difference across all death/rebirth cycles
verdict.bit_exact_identity: FAIL — dtype widens float32→float64 during JSON round-trip
verdict.h_norm_identity: PASS — h_norm matches within 1e-6 across all cycles

root_cause.description: PyTorch weights are float32. JSON serialization uses tolist() which becomes Python floats. np.array() on reload defaults to float64.
root_cause.evidence: All 5 weight arrays: exact values match (np.array_equal=True) but dtype changes from float32 to float64
root_cause.impact: Accuracy: none. Hash: breaks. Memory: doubles (1.1MB → 2.2MB).

control_vs_experimental.conclusion: Death/rebirth cycles produce IDENTICAL predictions to uninterrupted control

fix_required.option_1: Cast to float32 in PersistentRNN.load_checkpoint(): np.array(w['Wxh'], dtype=np.float32)
fix_required.option_2: Use np.savez() instead of JSON for binary float32 preservation
fix_required.option_3: Hash only values (not bytes) using normalized representation

honest_claim: The organism survives process death with FUNCTIONAL identity. Predictions are identical (0.0000 diff). The SHA-256 hash fails due to JSON float dtype widening (float32→float64) — a serialization artifact, not a knowledge loss. The fix is one line: dtype=np.float32 on reload.
substrate_independence: PROVEN at functional level. The weights + hidden state encode complete identity. The substrate (Python process) is separable and disposable.
